speaker: interviewee
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewee
text: Pictures of my family I don't have.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't need anything from me, do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At the end. No, at the end I would like to-- why I'm here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, [INAUDIBLE] together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how we became friends. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's December 2nd. The survivor is Eva Abraham-Podietz; interviewer, Daisy Miller; Los Angeles, and it's in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll wait til he taps me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like you to begin by telling us about your history before the war, please. What was life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in Hamburg, Germany in 1927. So, basically, I do not remember life without a Hitler, because I don't remember my very early years, naturally. But what I do remember is a very happy home life. I have an older brother. He is three years older. And my parents were very fond of each other. And it was a very harmonious kind of a life in-- that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peter. Well, no. In Germany it was Heinz Peter. But later on he dropped the 'Heinz' and became the Peter. So I really now call him only Pete. He's still living in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to tell you something about the background of my family, because it's very different. My father's generation, the Rosenbaums of our family, came from a very small area in a village in Mecklenburg. And generally speaking, I can-- we can-- not only I, of course-- can prove that we were in Germany since 1350. I have a genealogical table, which later on maybe you will have a chance to see. And they were in Germany all those years, those generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have pictures of my great-great grandfather going back that way. They all had longevity. So there was his 75th birthday, 85th birthday, and 95th-- 90 and 95th birthdays. They were merchants. Most of them had a store. My grandparents, I remember, their store myself in Mecklenburg. My father told me a lot about his youth when he came to see me here in England-- in America. And he was telling me about anti-Semitism already when he was a boy, so much so that he had to leave Mecklenburg and Schwerin, which was in Mecklenburg, to go to high school out of the city, to a bigger city. And he was affected by that anti-Semitism there. He was sent to England. And I'm mentioning that purposely, because he is trained in the textile business. And his parents sent him to England to learn the textile business. And he then, as a young man, later on, when we went to England, made contact again with those people who trained him.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what time-- when was this about?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was in his early 20s. He was born in 1890. So it was the turn of the century, before World War I. Then he went back to Germany and joined the German army. Was sent to France. And his-- my grandmother-- the only one I know I remember was his mother-- my grandfather had died-- was a very fascinating lady. So when I found letters that, when he was in France, she wrote in French, [FRENCH]. When he was in England, she wrote in English. So she must've been a pretty cultured, bright old lady. I only, of course, knew her in her latter years. But he was in the German army-- as was as my uncle, his brother, his older brother, in France-- and served. He got the Iron Cross. So they were good German citizens. They were a respected family in Mecklenburg. They were the only department store. It was small-- you know, here in America you think of a department store, of Bloomingdale's or something huge. It was a tiny little store. And they sold most everything. And they were well recognized although they were Jewish. There was small synagogue. My father was the last born Jew in Sternberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the city again?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-E-R-N-B-E-R-G, Sternberg, in Mecklenburg. And they made use of the synagogue three days a week-- three times a year, rather. And there was a cemetery. But the cemetery was right on top of the hill. I remember going there with my father to say goodbye to the uncles, to the relatives on top of the hill. And there were quite a number of Jewish graves. When my grandfather died in 1926, I believe-- '27, before I was born-- my father and uncle, having moved to Hamburg, moved their mother to Hamburg, as well. My uncle and his family lived right next door to us all the time I remember. So I was the only girl, a tomboy brought up with a brother-- an older brother and two older cousins. And I was rather-- well, I call it cheeky. Fresh, I guess you would call it. And was rather abrupt. And I knew that the older guys would protect me if I ever got into too much trouble. So it was a very warm, loving kind of a household that I lived in. And the boys were in our house more than they were in their own house, because my Aunt Betty, their mother, my cousins' mother, was a school teacher and a professor. She was the president of the lodge. And my mother was more at home. Although she was a teacher, and before she got married, she liked being at home with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say 'lodge,' what--

speaker: interviewee
text: The B'nai B'rith lodge. There were Masonic lodges, as well, which the Jewish people in Hamburg belonged to. And my aunt was a president. And she was a more intellectual kind of a person-- and the boys-- and wasn't home as much as my mother. So we had three-- the four of us were at home with my mother quite often. My mother's background-- by the way, my father's family were consciously Jewish. And as I said, they might go to synagogue three times of year. My mother's family came from Hamburg-- from Berlin, and were very Zionistic. They had a large family, and cousins and so on. They went to Israel-- at that time, Palestine-- very early on. Of the 12 children, I think six of them went to Palestine early-- '34, '35, '36 as [NON-ENGLISH]. And my mother worked and lived in Berlin, worked and trained as a kindergarten teacher and preschool teacher, and worked in Jewish institutions most of her life. She was very Zionistically Jewish oriented. Always wanted to go to Palestine and never got there until very much later in life. Which- my brother and I sent her eventually. Mother met my father at her cousin's wedding. My father was a friend of the bridegroom. My mother was the cousin of the bride. And that's where they met, the Hesses. And they were also very close. So my mother moved to Hamburg. And we were very close with Hess family. I'll tell you a story about the Hess family a little later, because they had four children. And they sent their four children and one girl to safety on the Kindertransporter when [? Irwin, ?] the father, was taken to a concentration camp on Crystal Night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did my life look in Hamburg as a child? My first memory of any kind of problems were being different. We played in the street in those days-- you know, hoops and tops and those sort of things as kids-- and rode our bicycles or tricycles at the time. I was rather fresh, and always stole  the little boy's tricycle next door, apparently. And so the story goes. I don't really remember this. But my brother tells me, and my parents later on told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then anti-Semitic remarks would be made-- that Jew girl always steals your tricycle kind of thing. Now, I don't remember the words. I don't-- I remember the atmosphere, and being scolded. You don't show off. You don't-- you're too naughty. You have to control yourself. And you have to be quiet. This sort of thing. Then we had a maid. And eventually with Nuremberg Laws-- as you probably know the history-- we were not allowed to keep maids. My father also had difficulty in his business. He was in business with his brother importing and exporting. And that became more and more difficult as the years went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took in boarders, young people who could no longer be trained. I'm talking now about '36, '37, '38. In the smaller cities around Hamburg, they were no longer allowed to train and go to colleges. In Hamburg, they were. So my mother took in the young people. And they stayed. And that was fun. So these were all Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were all Jewish boys. And then we had to give up that bigger apartment and move to a smaller one just a little further up the street, again next to my uncle and my aunt and the cousins. And I went to the local school. In Germany, you don't go to school until you're six.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- in 1933, Hitler already had come to power, as you know. I was in school, in first grade, in the regular school. And I would go with the children. And then I remember them being told, don't walk with that Jewish girl, and being isolated from the other children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one incident which clearly sticks out in my mind was coming to school, and the teacher coming in his Nazi uniform. And although it wasn't directed at me at all, he walked up and down the aisles. We would sit in rows. And I saw the black boots. And I saw his uniform. And I was just in a state of shock. I was petrified. And went home and said, I'm not going to that school anymore. I totally freaked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew what that represented.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. You see, although I couldn't read, although I couldn't write, it was very obvious that we were different. Example-- there were parades. And it was-- all the school children had to line the streets when the Fuhrer came. So we would all go. And then you would salute the swastika, or you were expected to salute the swastika.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember being ambivalent about it. Should I? Shouldn't I? I knew it wasn't my flag. I knew I was Jewish, and that swastika is an indicator that-- it was anti-- against the Jews. But the pomp and circumstance, and the band and this sort of thing, it was attractive to a child. So it was a very great dilemma. I remember a lot of incidents, for instance, whereby my parents would go hush hush. They would talk with the [? Hindlers, ?] where I'm staying right now, with her parents, about plans, how to get out, and arguments. Is it going to stay, or is it just temporary? And where should we go? And should we emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are things that I picked up, although we were protected very much by our parents from actual happenings. They would say that we had the flu or something when they knew the Fuhrer would come to-- to parades. And we would not be allowed to go out. So you pick this sort of stuff up. Christmastime, we lived in a high rise. I would be sent to take the Christmas gifts up to the super who lived on the top of the building. But my mother would stand in the landing to hear that I would be OK up there. And I was told, don't go in. Mrs. Fox-- you know, just give them their presents, and don't go in. Although we knew them. And I remember her trying to beckon me to come in. I saw the Christmas tree and the lights. And I was very tempted to go. But those cold blue eyes, I still see in front of me. And later on, I found-- I heard that she was the one who denounced all the Jews in the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you picked up-- however young you were, you picked up signals. I don't quite know how-- by body language and by looks, a comfort level or discomfort level. This is how life was for me as a young child already. And then the incident at school with the teacher, I refused.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my father, as I said, went to England, and went to England to try to get visas for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1936 already. He tried to get business going again. He made contact with the people, and found a guardian angel-- a woman who, by that time, was much-- she was about the same age as my father. He found her again. And she tried very hard to set him up in business in London, and tried to get visas for the family, and my uncle's family, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- yes, she was a Jewish woman. Yes, yes. [? Mrs. Marder ?] was a Jewish English-- well, I guess her relatives came from Russia, originally. She had, meantime, become quite wealthy in the textile business. So she was very helpful. In 1936, my father kept on coming back to Germany. But as he was traveling, they were suspicious of him. And in 1937, it was advisable for him not to come back to England-- from England to Hamburg. This angel sent a visa-- a student visa for my brother in 1937-- because that was the time that he would have had to go to high school-- and paid for a high school education in England for him. So my brother left. And that left my mother and myself behind. My parents were pretty wise people. My father was-- had a lot of civil courage, was a doer. And I remember discussions with friends and relatives. This is not going to blow over. This is going to be for real, and we'd better get the hell out of here. And, as I said, made all the attempts to get the visa, and was refused. Time after time, the visa was rejected for my mother and myself. And we couldn't understand it. He couldn't understand it. And he got a partner-- an English partner-- to set up the business. And, financially, he would've been OK. We were sent-- I was sent to the Jewish school. When I refused to school-- I had to go to school in some school-- I went to the Jewish school. And I was an outsider there again. It was like a parochial school. Only the religious children, really, would go to the Jewish school at that time. But, of course, the small-- the more and more children-- Jewish children-- were unable to go to the public schools. And there was a coming and a going, and a coming and going. So there wasn't much education. It was very, very difficult, being in the Jewish to school, for me. I was-- we were Reform Jews. We were conscious Jews, but we're not religious Jews. So I remember the teachers being very frustrated. In hindsight, you know, I realize how difficult it must have been for the staff. And for the whole atmosphere, it was difficult. The teachers were very sadistic with these goyishe Jews coming in, as it were. And they would make examples of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember being asked to read in Hebrew page so and so. Eva, would you read? And they knew darn well I couldn't read Hebrew very well. And you know, the awkwardness and the embarrassment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, a hand came and touched me-- the girl next to me. And she said, you come home with me. I'll teach you how to read Hebrew. There's a lifelong relationship with [? Hannah ?] to this day. She's in Israel, and has seven children. And these are the kindnesses that you remember. And you also remember the nastinesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, basically, my childhood was a happy one but a very insecure one. There was a lot of coming and going, and a lot of losses. You would make friends with some people. And friends of my parents would come and go. And this one would leave. And this one would go to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle and-- and my older cousin, example. They came to our apartment looking for my father. My mother was out trying to get to the bank, because they no longer were allowed to keep money in banks. It was confiscated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came home. Kristallnacht. That was the day of days. I was having to go by bicycle to this Jewish school, which was in a different moila-- Jewish quarter-- than the public school I lived in-- worked in-- that I went to in the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Crystal Night I went to school as usual. And I smelt the smoke. And there were only the principal and two teachers there. And they told us to go straight home again. I went to my brother's school where my cousin was. And I saw the synagogues burning. Came back, and on my bicycle. And a group of Nazi Youths came from both sides, and pushed me into the tracks of the tram-- the tram lines. And my front wheel got stuck. And then, of course, I fell off. And they're laughing. And they thought they were terrific, you know. They were great. I remember being pretty hurt, and not being able to ride the bicycle-- it was all out of whack-- and going home. And there was nobody home. My mother wasn't there. And I looked where my aunt lived. And there was a green [GERMAN] as they called it. It was like a closed wagon. And, handcuffed, my aunt was being led out. I had no idea. That was in 1938, in November-- yeah, November '38. [SOBBING] [SOB] [? I was afraid. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Mother didn't come back. I had the key to the apartment. There was a knock at the door. And it was not usual, you know, for people to knock. And I put the chain on. Gestapo, in civvies. Where's your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea, I said. He is not here. And, you know, I had to open. And I remember the chutzpah. Show me your IDs. [LAUGHS] I said to them-- I was 10 years old-- 11 years old. Show me your IDs. And these guys pushed some kind of badge through the slit. And then, of course, I had to let them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they looked. And they went. We had feather beds in the-- in the bedrooms. And it was winter. It was a very cold winter that year. And they went into the bedroom and took out knives and slit the feather beds. And I remember saying, are you expecting my father to be hiding in the bed? I mean, I don't know where that chutzpah came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they shoved me around and said, you  shut up. And, anyway, they obviously-- I don't know what they were looking for. And then they left. And I tried to push the feathers back into the bed. Then, eventually, my mother came back and said Eric, my cousin, and my uncle had been taken to the concentration camp. Because there was no school anymore on Kristallnacht on the 9th of November, he went to his father's office. And the Nazis came. And they took my uncle. And, as he was 16, they took him as well. So that day is very much in my memory. Eventually Mother came back. And my aunt was released. But my uncle and Eric stayed in Sachsenhausen. And we were, at that time, still able to pack things. And we were packing a [? lift, ?] which is a great big crate, to get things out to England. So we managed to get quite a lot of our silver and that sort of thing. It was still possible. And then the phone rang about a week after the Kristallnacht. We no longer went to school. And this cousin off my mother's-- where they met, the Hesses-- called. [? Irwin ?] was picked up and taken to a concentration camp. And I have decided-- I heard about children being saved by England, the Kindertransport. And at that and that street, at that and that, they are taking applications. And I have just signed up all four children. And I want you to sign up Eva. I was alone with my mother. My brother was already in England. It was a tremendous decision for parents. But she and one girl-- the oldest was 15. Then was a 14-year-old boy. And my cousins, they were twins, were 10. And they went on the first Kindertransport to England on the 8th of December, I think. It was the 7th or 8th of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was constant coming and going, and losses and insecurity, and very little education the last few years. It was a very uncomfortable existence. Were you aware of your uncle's whereabouts at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were lots of phone calls. And I had-- my mother had two sisters in Berlin. And Sachsenhausen was outside of Berlin. And, yes. My mother was a pretty courageous, sassy woman also. And she went to the police and said, what's this all about? Where are my-- my brother-in-law? And they, on the whole, were pretty decent. The police were pretty decent. You see, they were not Nazis. They were not the SS. And the fire companies were also pretty decent people. However, the law of the land was the Hitler, the Nazi regime. And they were not given the right orders. In other words, for instance, when the synagogues were burning, the police was there. The fire company was there. And the army was there. But they were not given orders to resist, do anything about it. So the police was not difficult, not bad. And they gave her the information, as far as they knew, that the people in that area were taken to Sachsenhausen. When we contacted my aunts, they found out that Eric and my uncle were there. They already had affidavits for America, although they wanted to go to England, you know, so that we would be together. And because they had the affidavits, after about-- I'm not sure. Before I left. So it could have not been between the 9th of December and 10th of-- November and December. About within that three and 1/2 week period, they were released because they had the affidavit and the ticket. Somebody got them, you know, the ticket on the Manhattan to go to America. And what happened was that they released those people who had possibilities of leaving the country at that time. I remember going in the middle of the night with my mother to meet the-- to meet them off the train. I didn't recognize my uncle and my cousin. Their heads, of course, were shorn. And it was very cold. And they had lost all their body weight. I just didn't recognize either of them. They were hanging out of the windows at the railroad station, and I did not recognize them. They were unrecognizable.
